questions.questionText: The nerve injured in fracture of the neck of fibula is:
questions.explanation: The common peroneal (fibular) nerve winds around the neck of fibula superficially, making it the most commonly injured nerve of the lower limb. Injury causes foot drop (loss of dorsiflexion and eversion). Tibial nerve is deep in the popliteal fossa. Sural nerve is a pure sensory nerve. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Lateral Compartment of Leg.

questions.questionText: Foot drop is caused by injury to which nerve?
questions.explanation: Common peroneal nerve → deep peroneal nerve (dorsiflexors: tibialis anterior, EHL, EDL) + superficial peroneal nerve (evertors: peroneus longus and brevis). Injury causes foot drop (loss of dorsiflexion) + high-stepping gait. Tibial nerve injury causes loss of plantarflexion. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Leg Compartments.

questions.questionText: The great saphenous vein drains into the:
questions.explanation: The great (long) saphenous vein (longest vein in body) drains into the femoral vein at the saphenous opening (fossa ovalis), 4 cm below and lateral to the pubic tubercle. The small saphenous vein drains into the popliteal vein. Memory aid: Great → Femoral, Small → Popliteal. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Lower Limb Veins.

questions.questionText: The nerve accompanying the great saphenous vein is:
questions.explanation: Saphenous nerve (branch of femoral nerve, L3-L4) accompanies the great saphenous vein below the knee. It is the largest purely sensory branch of the femoral nerve. It is at risk during saphenous vein stripping. The sural nerve accompanies the small saphenous vein. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Cutaneous Nerves.

questions.questionText: A 60-year-old patient presents with inability to extend the knee after a fall. Which structure is most likely injured?
questions.explanation: Inability to extend the knee = quadriceps mechanism injury. In elderly patients, quadriceps tendon rupture is more common (as opposed to patellar fracture in younger patients). The quadriceps is supplied by femoral nerve (L2-L4). Hamstrings are knee flexors. ACL prevents anterior tibial displacement. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Knee Joint.

questions.questionText: The ligament that prevents anterior displacement of tibia on femur is:
questions.explanation: ACL prevents anterior tibial displacement (anterior drawer test positive when torn). PCL prevents posterior tibial displacement (posterior drawer test). MCL resists valgus stress. LCL resists varus stress. Memory aid: ACL = Anterior tibial displacement prevented. ACL tears are common in sports (pivot injuries). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Knee Ligaments.

questions.questionText: The 'unhappy triad' of O'Donoghue involves injury to all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: O'Donoghue's unhappy/terrible triad: ACL + MCL + Medial meniscus injury. Occurs due to valgus force + lateral rotation of femur on fixed tibia (common in football). PCL is NOT part of this triad. The medial meniscus is attached to the MCL (unlike lateral meniscus-LCL), making it more prone to injury. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Knee Joint.

questions.questionText: The nerve supplying the adductor compartment of the thigh is:
questions.explanation: Obturator nerve (L2-L4, anterior division of lumbar plexus) supplies the adductor compartment (adductor longus, adductor brevis, adductor magnus — adductor part, gracilis, obturator externus). Femoral nerve supplies anterior compartment. Sciatic nerve supplies posterior compartment. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Thigh Compartments.

questions.questionText: Trendelenburg sign is positive due to paralysis of:
questions.explanation: Trendelenburg sign: when standing on affected side, the opposite (unsupported) hip drops due to weakness of gluteus medius and minimus (supplied by superior gluteal nerve, L4-S1). These muscles stabilize the pelvis during single-leg stance. Gluteus maximus (inferior gluteal nerve) extends and laterally rotates the hip. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Gluteal Region.

questions.questionText: The nerve that lies on the piriformis muscle and exits through the greater sciatic foramen below it is:
questions.explanation: The sciatic nerve (L4-S3) exits the pelvis through the greater sciatic foramen below the piriformis muscle. Superior gluteal nerve exits above the piriformis (only structure to do so). Pudendal nerve also exits below piriformis but then leaves the gluteal region through the lesser sciatic foramen. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Gluteal Region.

questions.questionText: Intracapsular fracture of the neck of femur causes avascular necrosis because:
questions.optionB: Retinacular vessels (medial circumflex femoral) are torn
questions.optionC: Nutrient artery of femur is damaged
questions.explanation: The femoral head receives blood supply mainly from retinacular arteries (branches of medial circumflex femoral artery), which ascend along the neck under the capsule. Intracapsular fractures tear these vessels, leading to AVN. Ligamentum teres artery is insignificant in adults. This is why intracapsular fractures are treated with replacement, not fixation, in elderly. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Hip Joint Blood Supply.

questions.questionText: The most commonly dislocated tarsal bone is:
questions.explanation: Talus is the most commonly dislocated tarsal bone. It has no muscle attachments, and 60% of its surface is covered by articular cartilage, making its blood supply precarious. Talar fracture/dislocation can cause AVN. Calcaneus is the most commonly fractured tarsal bone (fall from height). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Ankle and Foot.

questions.questionText: The structure passing through the adductor hiatus is:
questions.optionA: Femoral vein and profunda femoris artery
questions.optionD: Femoral artery, femoral vein, and nerve to vastus medialis
questions.explanation: The adductor hiatus (opening in adductor magnus) transmits the femoral artery (becoming popliteal artery) and femoral vein from the adductor canal to the popliteal fossa. The saphenous nerve exits the adductor canal by piercing its roof, NOT through the hiatus. The profunda femoris does not pass through. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Adductor Canal.

questions.questionText: The Locking mechanism of the knee involves:
questions.optionA: Lateral rotation of femur on tibia in last 30° of extension
questions.optionB: Medial rotation of femur on tibia in last 30° of extension
questions.optionC: Lateral rotation of tibia on femur in last 30° of extension
questions.optionD: Medial rotation of tibia on femur in last 30° of extension
questions.explanation: During the screw-home mechanism (locking), the femur medially rotates on the tibia in the last 30° of extension (when foot is on the ground). This is because the medial condyle articular surface is longer. Popliteus 'unlocks' by laterally rotating the femur on the fixed tibia. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Knee Biomechanics.

questions.questionText: The artery at greatest risk in posterior dislocation of the knee is:
questions.explanation: The popliteal artery is the deepest structure in the popliteal fossa, tethered above and below, making it vulnerable in posterior knee dislocation. Popliteal artery injury occurs in ~30% of knee dislocations. Always check distal pulses (dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial) after knee dislocation. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Popliteal Fossa.

questions.questionText: The injured structure when a rugby player sustains a blow to the lateral side of the knee (valgus stress) is:
questions.explanation: Valgus stress (force from lateral side) stretches and tears the MCL (tibial/medial collateral ligament). Varus stress tears the LCL. MCL is firmly attached to the medial meniscus — hence the unhappy triad (MCL + ACL + medial meniscus). LCL is NOT attached to the lateral meniscus. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Knee Ligaments.

questions.questionText: The muscle that prevents the forward dislocation of the femoral head during squatting is:
questions.explanation: Obturator internus acts as a dynamic 'check ligament' preventing anterior dislocation of the femoral head during squatting (hip in flexion, abduction, and external rotation). Piriformis acts as a lateral rotator but doesn't have this specific stabilizing role. Gluteus maximus extends the hip. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Hip Joint.

questions.questionText: The nerve that supplies the skin over the medial side of the leg and foot is:
questions.explanation: Saphenous nerve (terminal branch of femoral nerve) supplies the skin on the medial side of leg all the way to the medial border of the foot. It is the only nerve from the femoral nerve that crosses the knee. Sural nerve supplies the lateral side of leg and foot. Deep peroneal supplies the first web space. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Cutaneous Supply of Leg.

questions.questionText: The type of joint at the hip is:
questions.explanation: Hip joint is a ball and socket (spheroidal/enarthrodial) joint — the femoral head (ball) articulates with the acetabulum (socket). It allows movement in all axes: flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, rotation, circumduction. It is the most stable joint in the body due to bony, ligamentous, and muscular support. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Hip Joint.

questions.questionText: The dorsalis pedis artery is a continuation of:
questions.explanation: The anterior tibial artery becomes the dorsalis pedis artery at the ankle (at the level of the extensor retinaculum). It is palpated lateral to the extensor hallucis longus tendon. It is an important pulse for peripheral vascular assessment. Posterior tibial artery is palpated behind the medial malleolus. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Arteries of Leg.

questions.questionText: Injury to the superior gluteal nerve causes paralysis of:
questions.explanation: Superior gluteal nerve (L4-S1) supplies gluteus medius, gluteus minimus, and tensor fasciae latae. It is the only structure exiting above the piriformis in the greater sciatic foramen. Inferior gluteal nerve supplies gluteus maximus. Nerve to quadratus femoris supplies quadratus femoris and inferior gemellus. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Gluteal Nerves.

questions.questionText: The spring ligament (plantar calcaneonavicular ligament) supports which arch of the foot?
questions.explanation: The spring ligament supports the head of talus and maintains the medial longitudinal arch. Damage to it causes flat foot (pes planus). The medial arch is the more important arch (shock absorption during walking). Long plantar ligament supports the lateral arch. Tibialis posterior tendon is the most important dynamic support. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Foot Arches.

questions.questionText: The femoral nerve arises from which nerve roots?
questions.explanation: Femoral nerve (L2-L4, posterior divisions of lumbar plexus) is the largest branch of the lumbar plexus. It supplies the anterior compartment of thigh (quadriceps, sartorius, iliopsoas) and sensation to anterior thigh + medial leg (saphenous nerve). It enters the thigh deep to the inguinal ligament, lateral to the femoral artery. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Lumbar Plexus.

questions.questionText: During intramuscular injection in the gluteal region, the safe quadrant is:
questions.explanation: The upper outer (superolateral) quadrant of the gluteal region is the safe zone for IM injections because the sciatic nerve and gluteal vessels are located in the other quadrants. Injection in the wrong quadrant can damage the sciatic nerve, causing foot drop and posterior thigh sensory loss. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Gluteal Region Applied Anatomy.

questions.questionText: Damage to the deep peroneal nerve causes loss of sensation in:
questions.explanation: Deep peroneal nerve supplies the first web space (between great toe and second toe) — its autonomous sensory zone. It also supplies the dorsiflexors (tibialis anterior, EHL, EDL) and the intrinsic foot muscle EDB. Superficial peroneal supplies the rest of the dorsum. Sole = medial/lateral plantar nerves (tibial). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Leg Nerves.

questions.questionText: The calcaneal (Achilles) tendon is formed by the tendons of:
questions.explanation: The Achilles tendon is formed by the merging of gastrocnemius and soleus (together called triceps surae); plantaris tendon lies along it but does not contribute significantly. It is the strongest and thickest tendon in the body. Rupture (Thompson/Simmonds test positive) occurs classically in weekend warriors with sudden forceful dorsiflexion. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Posterior Leg.

questions.questionText: March fracture occurs in which metatarsal?
questions.explanation: March (stress/fatigue) fracture most commonly affects the 2nd metatarsal shaft. It is named after military recruits who develop it from prolonged marching. The 2nd metatarsal is the longest and bears maximum stress. Jones fracture affects the base of the 5th metatarsal. Avulsion fracture of the 5th metatarsal base is from peroneus brevis pull. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Foot Bones.

questions.questionText: A 70-year-old woman falls and presents with shortened, externally rotated lower limb with inability to bear weight. The most likely diagnosis is:
questions.explanation: Classic presentation of intracapsular fracture of the neck of femur in elderly: shortened and externally rotated leg (due to pull of lateral rotators), inability to bear weight, tenderness in groin. Intertrochanteric fractures show more shortening and rotation. Posterior hip dislocation shows flexion, adduction, and internal rotation. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Hip Joint Applied.

questions.questionText: The structure that passes through the lesser sciatic foramen is:
questions.explanation: Structures passing through the lesser sciatic foramen: internal pudendal artery/vein, pudendal nerve, and tendon of obturator internus. The pudendal nerve exits through the greater sciatic foramen below piriformis, hooks around the ischial spine, and re-enters the pelvis through the lesser sciatic foramen. Sciatic nerve exits only through the greater sciatic foramen. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Pelvic Foramina.
